1	# Data Description
2	
3	This document describes the two imaging modalities and the properties of the
4	coregistration problem, with concrete numbers measured from the 6 benchmark
5	subjects (755252, 767018, 767022, 782149, 788406, 790322). Numbers come from
6	the analysis in `code/notebooks/01_benchmark_data_analysis.ipynb`
7	and `code/sessions/01_analyze_benchmark_data/`.
8	
9	## Modalities
10	
11	### 1) 2p GCaMP (Source Sub-volume)
12	- In-vivo 2-photon cortical z-stack (CZStack).
13	- Image size: 512 × 512 × ~450 planes.
14	- Field of view options (from `manual workflow/step_1_process_files.ipynb`):
15	  400 um (XY resolution 0.78 um/pixel) or 700 um (1.37 um/pixel). All current
16	  benchmark subjects use 400 um.
17	- Z resolution: ~1 um/pixel.
18	- Physical extent (measured from centroids, per subject): CZ cells span
19	  ~392–398 um (x), ~392–396 um (y), ~404–424 um (z).
20	- Content: ~785–1016 GCaMP+ inhibitory neurons, with 3D ROI segmentation masks.
21	- From pia surface; the top ~50–110 um is blank buffer (no cells). Measured
22	  pia-plane offsets per subject: 55–108 um.
23	- Sub-volume embedded within the larger HCR volume.
24	
25	### 2) HCR Light-sheet (Target Volume)
26	- Ex-vivo HCR light-sheet imaging, matched to the in-vivo 2p volume (but
27	  larger).
28	- Raw XY resolution ≈ 0.245 um/pixel; Z resolution ≈ 1 um/pixel.
29	- **Cell centroids are stored at level-2 pyramid pixels** (`cell_centroids.npy`),
30	  where XY has been downsampled 4× from raw. This is the coordinate system the
31	  manual coregistration workflow operates in, using the conversion from
32	  `manual workflow/step_2_automatic_mapping_for_qc.ipynb`:
33	
34	  ```
35	  scale_x = dimensions.x[0] * 4e6   # ~0.98 um per level-2 pixel
36	  scale_y = dimensions.y[0] * 4e6
37	  scale_z = dimensions.z[0] * 1e6   # ~1 um per pixel
38	  ```
39	
40	- Level-2 effective resolution: ~0.980–0.989 um/pixel XY, ~1 um/pixel Z
41	  (slightly varies per subject; essentially isotropic in pixel space).
42	- Physical extent (level-2 centroids): ~1.8–2.3 mm XY, 0.88–1.34 mm Z.
43	  (The earlier "~1 × 1 × 0.5 mm" figure was based on raw-pixel volume, not the
44	  centroid coordinate space. The coregistration works in the centroid space.)
45	- Content per volume: ~39 k–127 k Rn28S-segmented cells (405 nm); 488-channel
46	  GFP+ subset ≈ 25 k–78 k at a 5-spot-per-cell threshold (matching the 2p
47	  GCaMP+ neurons).
48	- 3D ROI segmentation from Rn28S; 488-channel GFP quantification comes from
49	  one of three sources (same convention as
50	  `manual workflow/step_2_automatic_mapping_for_qc.ipynb`):
51	  1. pre-aggregated per-cell counts
52	     `{coreg_dir}/*_spot_488_counts.csv` (782149, 788406, 790322).
53	  2. raw per-spot CSV aggregated on the fly via `SEG_ID.value_counts()` and
54	     joined with `cell_body_segmentation/metrics.pickle` for volume/density
55	     (767018 and any subject that lacks the pre-aggregated file).
56	  3. per-cell mean intensity `data/cell_data_mean_{subj}_R1.csv` channel 488
57	     (755252, 767022). No spot count is available here, only a fluorescence
58	     intensity.
59	  A GFP+ cell is defined by `counts >= gfp_min_spots` (default 5) when a spot
60	  count exists. The loader exposes `gfp_min_spots` as a parameter so the
61	  threshold can be revised from later analyses. Intensity-only subjects use
62	  the raw `mean` and must be thresholded by the caller.
63	- Top part contains blank buffer. Thickness is unknown, varies across subjects
64	  (measured pia offsets: 30–410 um), and is **slanted** (measured pia-plane
65	  tilt: 1.2°–11.6°, with 782149 the most tilted).
66	
67	## Benchmark-subject statistics
68	
69	Measured per-subject values (all coordinates converted to physical microns).
70	
71	GFP+ counts below use the default 5-spot threshold where applicable.
72	
73	| subject | CZ cells | HCR total | HCR GFP+ (source, threshold)          | coreg matched | match rate |
74	|---------|---------:|----------:|---------------------------------------|--------------:|-----------:|
75	| 755252  | 835      | 84 233    | 77 785 (intensity_r1, no threshold)   | 639           | 76.5%      |
76	| 767018  | 785      | 108 506   | 58 959 (aggregated_spots_csv, ≥5)     | 273           | 34.8%      |
77	| 767022  | 926      | 76 336    | 72 213 (intensity_r1, no threshold)   | 793           | 85.6%      |
78	| 782149  | 894      | 39 291    | 25 251 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 303           | 33.9%      |
79	| 788406  | 932      | 127 275   | 69 680 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 787           | 84.4%      |
80	| 790322  | 1016     | 106 379   | 72 532 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 778           | 76.6%      |
81	
82	Notes:
83	- 767018 lacks the pre-aggregated `spot_488_counts.csv` but has the raw
84	  `image_spot_detection/channel_488_spots/spots.csv` (4.86 M puncta). The
85	  loader aggregates it per `SEG_ID` at load time. At a 5-spot threshold,
86	  76 607 cells with ≥1 spot reduce to 58 959 GFP+.
87	- For 755252/767022 the "GFP+" figure is every HCR cell with a non-zero 488
88	  intensity measurement (no spot threshold available). A downstream intensity
89	  threshold is required to obtain a comparable GFP+ count.
90	
91	## Key relationship between 2p and HCR
92	
93	- CZ sub-volume is localized inside HCR.
94	- **XY rotation is ~180°** around the z-axis (measured: angles −177° to +180°,
95	  always near 180° in absolute value; this is not a flip).
96	- Both start from the cortex surface (pia at top, deep cortex at bottom).
97	- ROI segmentations have errors in both modalities, so ROI-to-ROI is not 1:1.
98	
99	## Transform characterisation
100	
101	Measured from active landmarks (500–800 per subject, final QC'd iteration).
102	An anisotropic similarity is fit (rotation + diagonal scale + translation).
103	
104	### Anisotropic expansion (CZ in-vivo → HCR ex-vivo, physical microns)
105	
106	| subject | XY expansion (mean) | Z expansion | XY:Z anisotropy |
107	|---------|--------------------:|------------:|----------------:|
108	| 755252  | 1.64×               | 2.13×       | 0.77            |
109	| 767018  | 1.70×               | 3.58×       | 0.47            |
110	| 767022  | 1.81×               | 2.49×       | 0.73            |
111	| 782149  | 1.92×               | 2.93×       | 0.66            |
112	| 788406  | 1.78×               | 2.82×       | 0.63            |
113	| 790322  | 1.77×               | 3.04×       | 0.58            |
114	
115	Expansion is consistently anisotropic: XY expands ~1.6–1.9×, Z expands ~2.1–3.6×
116	(Z is ~1.5× more expanded than XY). A single isotropic scale cannot align the
117	modalities.
118	
119	### Residual deformation after anisotropic affine (landmark RMS, microns)
120	
121	| subject | residual RMS | residual max |
122	|---------|-------------:|-------------:|
123	| 755252  | 27           | 83           |
124	| 767018  | 25           | 88           |
125	| 767022  | 16           | 53           |
126	| 782149  | 29           | 65           |
127	| 788406  | 43           | 114          |
128	| 790322  | 22           | 91           |
129	
130	After the affine fit, residual magnitudes reach ~25–45 um RMS, up to ~110 um
131	max. This is the scale of the nonrigid deformation the algorithm must handle
132	after affine alignment.
133	
134	### Anisotropy introduced by coordinate systems
135	
136	In addition to the physical expansion, the pixel coordinate systems themselves
137	are slightly anisotropic: CZ is 0.78 um/px XY vs 1 um/px Z, and HCR is ~0.988
138	um/px XY vs 1 um/px Z. When registering in pixel space, the RBF absorbs both
139	the physical expansion and this pixel-space anisotropy.
140	
141	## Pia surface and depth-from-surface coordinate
142	
143	Two surface-fitting methods were evaluated:
144	
145	1. **Image-based (primary)**: for each (y, x) column of the 3D image, find
146	   the first z at which intensity exceeds a per-column relative threshold
147	   `col_bg + 0.05 × (col_top − col_bg)` (`col_bg = 10-pct`,
148	   `col_top = 95-pct`). The low 5 % margin is essential — larger margins
149	   (25 % initially tried) skip the sparse outer tissue layer and anchor the
150	   plane in dense cortex. Require a ≥ 15 um contiguous above-threshold
151	   segment and reject columns whose dynamic range is below a minimum.
152	   IRLS-Huber plane fit with tile-median outlier rejection.
153	
154	   **For HCR** we use a **combined-channel volume**: load every available
155	   channel (405, 488, 561 or 514, 594), normalise each by its own
156	   background/percentile, and sum. Autofluorescence aligns across channels
157	   exactly at the tissue–buffer transition, producing a crisper boundary
158	   than any single channel. 488 alone is too sparse (~100–200 valid columns
159	   vs ~30 000); 405 alone works but can overshoot into dense cortex.
160	   Level 4 of the zarr pyramid (~4 um / voxel) is used.
161	
162	2. **Hybrid (default for HCR)**: Uses ROI segmentation data to constrain the
163	   image-based search window. Steps:
164	   (a) Density filter: keep only ROIs with ≥6 neighbors within 30 um (removes
165	       isolated out-of-tissue false positives).
166	   (b) Fit a plane to the minimum-z ROI per (x, y) tile (coarse prior
167	       `z_prior(x, y)`).
168	   (c) In the image-based search, restrict each column to z ∈
169	       [z_prior − 100 um, z_prior + 100 um] before finding the first-crossing.
170	   (d) Fit the final plane to the constrained first-crossing map.
171	   This exploits the ROI-based coarse localization as a guard against the
172	   image-based search getting confused by noise far from the tissue boundary.
173	   **It is the current default in `analyze_subject()`.**
174	
175	3. **Centroid-based (fallback)**: tile-top quantile of ROI centroids + IRLS-
176	   Huber plane fit. Kept for diagnostic comparison. It systematically
177	   underestimates the pia by 50–200 um because the topmost segmented ROIs
178	   already sit below the real tissue boundary.
179	
180	Fit quality is judged by the **fraction-above-pia**: share of cells whose
181	centroid lies > 5 um above the estimated pia. It should be small (<~10 %);
182	residual positives are out-of-tissue segmentation false positives or a thin
183	layer of pial/meningeal cells.
184	
185	### Hybrid fits (current default for HCR)
186	
187	| subject | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
188	|---------|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
189	| 755252  | 259        | 7.8          | 37             | 4.0 %          |
190	| 767018  | 130        | 6.6          | 37             | 2.5 %          |
191	| 767022  | 267        | 3.1          | 69             | 4.4 %          |
192	| 782149  | 6          | 11.4         | 35             | 5.7 %          |
193	| 788406  | 148        | 2.9          | 57             | 5.8 %          |
194	| 790322  | 236        | 3.7          | 47             | 8.2 %          |
195	
196	Hybrid is better than image-only for 767018 (10.8% → 2.5%) and 782149
197	(8.2% → 5.7%); slightly worse for 755252 and 790322 (< 2 pp regression).
198	The constrained search eliminates the case where the image-based fitter
199	wanders into a noise peak far above the tissue.
200	
201	### Image-based fits (for comparison)
202	
203	| subject | CZ c (um) | CZ tilt (°) | CZ rough (um) | CZ frac-above | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
204	|---------|---------:|------------:|--------------:|--------------:|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
205	| 755252  | 57       | 3.1         | 12            | 1.0 %         | 202        | 9.3          | 39             | 2.1 %          |
206	| 767018  | 46       | 1.9         | 4             | 0.0 %         | 238        | 6.5          | 106            | 10.8 %         |
207	| 767022  | 52       | 0.7         | 7             | 0.3 %         | 290        | 3.9          | 87             | 4.6 %          |
208	| 782149  | 51       | 2.0         | 10            | 3.8 %         | 18         | 11.1         | 45             | 8.2 %          |
209	| 788406  | 57       | 2.9         | 4             | 0.0 %         | 135        | 2.8          | 93             | 5.2 %          |
210	| 790322  | 50       | 1.0         | 3             | 0.0 %         | 169        | 3.2          | 89             | 6.6 %          |
211	
212	### Centroid-based fits (for comparison)
213	
214	| subject | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
215	|---------|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
216	| 755252  | 390        | 6.9          | 96             | 9.0 %          |
217	| 767018  | 315        | 5.0          | 124            | 11.2 %         |
218	| 767022  | 409        | 3.0          | 84             | 8.4 %          |
219	| 782149  | 30         | 11.6         | 94             | 15.7 %         |
220	| 788406  | 248        | 1.2          | 98             | 7.6 %          |
221	| 790322  | 256        | 4.0          | 85             | 8.5 %          |
222	
223	Observations:
224	- CZ pia offset is tight (46–57 um), tilt ≤ 3°, roughness ≤ 12 um — a
225	  plane fit on the 3D image is essentially perfect.
226	- HCR pia offset varies widely (18–290 um, the "slanted" remark is
227	  reflected in tilts up to ~11°). 782149 is the thinnest section — its
228	  small offset (c=18) means the zarr starts essentially at the pia.
229	- `fraction-above-pia` is 0–11 % for image-based fits, ~8–16 % for
230	  centroid-based fits. The remaining positives are mostly out-of-tissue
231	  segmentation false positives and a few meningeal cells just above the
232	  cortex proper.
233	- Depth-from-surface (`depth = z − z_surface(x, y)`) is a robust axial
234	  coordinate that removes buffer-thickness and pia-tilt confounds.
235	
236	## Candidate features for automatic coregistration
237	
238	Measured on all 6 subjects:
239	
240	- **Cell nearest-neighbor distances** (mean 1-NN, um):
241	  - CZ: 21.5–24.6; HCR all: 17.1–21.1; HCR GFP+: 18.6–25.0.
242	  - Characterises packing density; informs k-NN radii for matching.
243	- **Depth density profiles**: cells/20-um bin vs depth from pia, for CZ, HCR
244	  total, and HCR GFP+. Cross-correlation (after Z-expansion rescaling) provides
245	  a 1D axial localization cue. Peaks/troughs in density reflect cortical layer
246	  structure and can be used as landmarks.
247	- **Pia surface normal**: an initial-alignment cue independent of cell
248	  positions; the CZ and HCR surface normals should be aligned after the ~180°
249	  rotation.
250	- **GFP+ local density peaks**: candidate constellations for the manual
251	  workflow's "unique clustered pattern" step.
252	- **ROI volumes / morphology** (from HCR `cell_body_segmentation/metrics.pickle`
253	  and CZ segmentation masks): available for per-cell matching confidence.
254	
255	## Coordinate-system conventions (summary)
256	
257	| source | column order | units | notes |
258	|--------|--------------|-------|-------|
259	| CZ centroid CSV | `[czstack_cell_id, czstack_z, czstack_y, czstack_x]` | pixels (0.78 um XY, 1 um Z) | |
260	| HCR centroid NPY | `[z, y, x, id]` | pixels (HCR level-2 ~0.988 um XY, 1 um Z) | |
261	| Landmark CSV | `[ids, active, cz_x, cz_y, cz_z, hcr_x, hcr_y, hcr_z]` | pixels in each modality's native space | no header |
262	| `spot_488_counts.csv` | `[hcr_id, counts, volume, global_bbox, density]` | 488 GFP feature | only for 782149/788406/790322 |
263	| `cell_data_mean_*_R1.csv` | channel-indexed intensity means | raw intensity | GFP+ proxy for 755252/767022 |
264	| Coreg table | `[czstack_id, hcr_id]` | — | final mapping |
265	
266	## Data-format variations across subjects
267	
268	- **767018**: uses an older processing pipeline. No `fused_ng.json` (use the
269	  fallback resolution). HCR cell centroids are in
270	  `767018_HCR_cell_centroids.csv` in addition to
271	  `cell_body_segmentation/cell_centroids.npy`. GFP+ is derived by aggregating
272	  the raw `image_spot_detection/channel_488_spots/spots.csv` on `SEG_ID` at
273	  load time (no pre-aggregated `spot_488_counts.csv`).
274	- **755252, 767022**: no `spot_488_counts.csv`. Use
275	  `/root/capsule/data/cell_data_mean_{subject}_R1.csv` with channel 488 mean
276	  intensity as GFP+ proxy. Thresholding is required to obtain a true GFP+ set.
277	- **782149**: thinner HCR section (878 um Z vs 1160–1329 um for others);
278	  strongest pia tilt (~12°); lowest match rate (~34%).
279	- **Landmark file naming**: older subjects (755252, 767018, 767022) use a
280	  `_reordered_qced` suffix; newer ones use `_qced`. Benchmark loaders must
281	  handle both.
282	
283	## Testing priorities
284	
285	- Primary: **788406** and **790322** (highest-quality data, spot-based GFP+).
286	- Secondary: **767018** (older pipeline) and **782149** (thin section, tilted
287	  pia).
288

file.content:
# Data Description

This document describes the two imaging modalities and the properties of the
coregistration problem, with concrete numbers measured from the 6 benchmark
subjects (755252, 767018, 767022, 782149, 788406, 790322). Numbers come from
the analysis in `code/notebooks/01_benchmark_data_analysis.ipynb`
and `code/sessions/01_analyze_benchmark_data/`.

## Modalities

### 1) 2p GCaMP (Source Sub-volume)
- In-vivo 2-photon cortical z-stack (CZStack).
- Image size: 512 × 512 × ~450 planes.
- Field of view options (from `manual workflow/step_1_process_files.ipynb`):
  400 um (XY resolution 0.78 um/pixel) or 700 um (1.37 um/pixel). All current
  benchmark subjects use 400 um.
- Z resolution: ~1 um/pixel.
- Physical extent (measured from centroids, per subject): CZ cells span
  ~392–398 um (x), ~392–396 um (y), ~404–424 um (z).
- Content: ~785–1016 GCaMP+ inhibitory neurons, with 3D ROI segmentation masks.
- From pia surface; the top ~50–110 um is blank buffer (no cells). Measured
  pia-plane offsets per subject: 55–108 um.
- Sub-volume embedded within the larger HCR volume.

### 2) HCR Light-sheet (Target Volume)
- Ex-vivo HCR light-sheet imaging, matched to the in-vivo 2p volume (but
  larger).
- Raw XY resolution ≈ 0.245 um/pixel; Z resolution ≈ 1 um/pixel.
- **Cell centroids are stored at level-2 pyramid pixels** (`cell_centroids.npy`),
  where XY has been downsampled 4× from raw. This is the coordinate system the
  manual coregistration workflow operates in, using the conversion from
  `manual workflow/step_2_automatic_mapping_for_qc.ipynb`:

  ```
  scale_x = dimensions.x[0] * 4e6   # ~0.98 um per level-2 pixel
  scale_y = dimensions.y[0] * 4e6
  scale_z = dimensions.z[0] * 1e6   # ~1 um per pixel
  ```

- Level-2 effective resolution: ~0.980–0.989 um/pixel XY, ~1 um/pixel Z
  (slightly varies per subject; essentially isotropic in pixel space).
- Physical extent (level-2 centroids): ~1.8–2.3 mm XY, 0.88–1.34 mm Z.
  (The earlier "~1 × 1 × 0.5 mm" figure was based on raw-pixel volume, not the
  centroid coordinate space. The coregistration works in the centroid space.)
- Content per volume: ~39 k–127 k Rn28S-segmented cells (405 nm); 488-channel
  GFP+ subset ≈ 25 k–78 k at a 5-spot-per-cell threshold (matching the 2p
  GCaMP+ neurons).
- 3D ROI segmentation from Rn28S; 488-channel GFP quantification comes from
  one of three sources (same convention as
  `manual workflow/step_2_automatic_mapping_for_qc.ipynb`):
  1. pre-aggregated per-cell counts
     `{coreg_dir}/*_spot_488_counts.csv` (782149, 788406, 790322).
  2. raw per-spot CSV aggregated on the fly via `SEG_ID.value_counts()` and
     joined with `cell_body_segmentation/metrics.pickle` for volume/density
     (767018 and any subject that lacks the pre-aggregated file).
  3. per-cell mean intensity `data/cell_data_mean_{subj}_R1.csv` channel 488
     (755252, 767022). No spot count is available here, only a fluorescence
     intensity.
  A GFP+ cell is defined by `counts >= gfp_min_spots` (default 5) when a spot
  count exists. The loader exposes `gfp_min_spots` as a parameter so the
  threshold can be revised from later analyses. Intensity-only subjects use
  the raw `mean` and must be thresholded by the caller.
- Top part contains blank buffer. Thickness is unknown, varies across subjects
  (measured pia offsets: 30–410 um), and is **slanted** (measured pia-plane
  tilt: 1.2°–11.6°, with 782149 the most tilted).

## Benchmark-subject statistics

Measured per-subject values (all coordinates converted to physical microns).

GFP+ counts below use the default 5-spot threshold where applicable.

| subject | CZ cells | HCR total | HCR GFP+ (source, threshold)          | coreg matched | match rate |
|---------|---------:|----------:|---------------------------------------|--------------:|-----------:|
| 755252  | 835      | 84 233    | 77 785 (intensity_r1, no threshold)   | 639           | 76.5%      |
| 767018  | 785      | 108 506   | 58 959 (aggregated_spots_csv, ≥5)     | 273           | 34.8%      |
| 767022  | 926      | 76 336    | 72 213 (intensity_r1, no threshold)   | 793           | 85.6%      |
| 782149  | 894      | 39 291    | 25 251 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 303           | 33.9%      |
| 788406  | 932      | 127 275   | 69 680 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 787           | 84.4%      |
| 790322  | 1016     | 106 379   | 72 532 (spot_counts_csv, ≥5)          | 778           | 76.6%      |

Notes:
- 767018 lacks the pre-aggregated `spot_488_counts.csv` but has the raw
  `image_spot_detection/channel_488_spots/spots.csv` (4.86 M puncta). The
  loader aggregates it per `SEG_ID` at load time. At a 5-spot threshold,
  76 607 cells with ≥1 spot reduce to 58 959 GFP+.
- For 755252/767022 the "GFP+" figure is every HCR cell with a non-zero 488
  intensity measurement (no spot threshold available). A downstream intensity
  threshold is required to obtain a comparable GFP+ count.

## Key relationship between 2p and HCR

- CZ sub-volume is localized inside HCR.
- **XY rotation is ~180°** around the z-axis (measured: angles −177° to +180°,
  always near 180° in absolute value; this is not a flip).
- Both start from the cortex surface (pia at top, deep cortex at bottom).
- ROI segmentations have errors in both modalities, so ROI-to-ROI is not 1:1.

## Transform characterisation

Measured from active landmarks (500–800 per subject, final QC'd iteration).
An anisotropic similarity is fit (rotation + diagonal scale + translation).

### Anisotropic expansion (CZ in-vivo → HCR ex-vivo, physical microns)

| subject | XY expansion (mean) | Z expansion | XY:Z anisotropy |
|---------|--------------------:|------------:|----------------:|
| 755252  | 1.64×               | 2.13×       | 0.77            |
| 767018  | 1.70×               | 3.58×       | 0.47            |
| 767022  | 1.81×               | 2.49×       | 0.73            |
| 782149  | 1.92×               | 2.93×       | 0.66            |
| 788406  | 1.78×               | 2.82×       | 0.63            |
| 790322  | 1.77×               | 3.04×       | 0.58            |

Expansion is consistently anisotropic: XY expands ~1.6–1.9×, Z expands ~2.1–3.6×
(Z is ~1.5× more expanded than XY). A single isotropic scale cannot align the
modalities.

### Residual deformation after anisotropic affine (landmark RMS, microns)

| subject | residual RMS | residual max |
|---------|-------------:|-------------:|
| 755252  | 27           | 83           |
| 767018  | 25           | 88           |
| 767022  | 16           | 53           |
| 782149  | 29           | 65           |
| 788406  | 43           | 114          |
| 790322  | 22           | 91           |

After the affine fit, residual magnitudes reach ~25–45 um RMS, up to ~110 um
max. This is the scale of the nonrigid deformation the algorithm must handle
after affine alignment.

### Anisotropy introduced by coordinate systems

In addition to the physical expansion, the pixel coordinate systems themselves
are slightly anisotropic: CZ is 0.78 um/px XY vs 1 um/px Z, and HCR is ~0.988
um/px XY vs 1 um/px Z. When registering in pixel space, the RBF absorbs both
the physical expansion and this pixel-space anisotropy.

## Pia surface and depth-from-surface coordinate

Two surface-fitting methods were evaluated:

1. **Image-based (primary)**: for each (y, x) column of the 3D image, find
   the first z at which intensity exceeds a per-column relative threshold
   `col_bg + 0.05 × (col_top − col_bg)` (`col_bg = 10-pct`,
   `col_top = 95-pct`). The low 5 % margin is essential — larger margins
   (25 % initially tried) skip the sparse outer tissue layer and anchor the
   plane in dense cortex. Require a ≥ 15 um contiguous above-threshold
   segment and reject columns whose dynamic range is below a minimum.
   IRLS-Huber plane fit with tile-median outlier rejection.

   **For HCR** we use a **combined-channel volume**: load every available
   channel (405, 488, 561 or 514, 594), normalise each by its own
   background/percentile, and sum. Autofluorescence aligns across channels
   exactly at the tissue–buffer transition, producing a crisper boundary
   than any single channel. 488 alone is too sparse (~100–200 valid columns
   vs ~30 000); 405 alone works but can overshoot into dense cortex.
   Level 4 of the zarr pyramid (~4 um / voxel) is used.

2. **Hybrid (default for HCR)**: Uses ROI segmentation data to constrain the
   image-based search window. Steps:
   (a) Density filter: keep only ROIs with ≥6 neighbors within 30 um (removes
       isolated out-of-tissue false positives).
   (b) Fit a plane to the minimum-z ROI per (x, y) tile (coarse prior
       `z_prior(x, y)`).
   (c) In the image-based search, restrict each column to z ∈
       [z_prior − 100 um, z_prior + 100 um] before finding the first-crossing.
   (d) Fit the final plane to the constrained first-crossing map.
   This exploits the ROI-based coarse localization as a guard against the
   image-based search getting confused by noise far from the tissue boundary.
   **It is the current default in `analyze_subject()`.**

3. **Centroid-based (fallback)**: tile-top quantile of ROI centroids + IRLS-
   Huber plane fit. Kept for diagnostic comparison. It systematically
   underestimates the pia by 50–200 um because the topmost segmented ROIs
   already sit below the real tissue boundary.

Fit quality is judged by the **fraction-above-pia**: share of cells whose
centroid lies > 5 um above the estimated pia. It should be small (<~10 %);
residual positives are out-of-tissue segmentation false positives or a thin
layer of pial/meningeal cells.

### Hybrid fits (current default for HCR)

| subject | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
|---------|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
| 755252  | 259        | 7.8          | 37             | 4.0 %          |
| 767018  | 130        | 6.6          | 37             | 2.5 %          |
| 767022  | 267        | 3.1          | 69             | 4.4 %          |
| 782149  | 6          | 11.4         | 35             | 5.7 %          |
| 788406  | 148        | 2.9          | 57             | 5.8 %          |
| 790322  | 236        | 3.7          | 47             | 8.2 %          |

Hybrid is better than image-only for 767018 (10.8% → 2.5%) and 782149
(8.2% → 5.7%); slightly worse for 755252 and 790322 (< 2 pp regression).
The constrained search eliminates the case where the image-based fitter
wanders into a noise peak far above the tissue.

### Image-based fits (for comparison)

| subject | CZ c (um) | CZ tilt (°) | CZ rough (um) | CZ frac-above | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
|---------|---------:|------------:|--------------:|--------------:|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
| 755252  | 57       | 3.1         | 12            | 1.0 %         | 202        | 9.3          | 39             | 2.1 %          |
| 767018  | 46       | 1.9         | 4             | 0.0 %         | 238        | 6.5          | 106            | 10.8 %         |
| 767022  | 52       | 0.7         | 7             | 0.3 %         | 290        | 3.9          | 87             | 4.6 %          |
| 782149  | 51       | 2.0         | 10            | 3.8 %         | 18         | 11.1         | 45             | 8.2 %          |
| 788406  | 57       | 2.9         | 4             | 0.0 %         | 135        | 2.8          | 93             | 5.2 %          |
| 790322  | 50       | 1.0         | 3             | 0.0 %         | 169        | 3.2          | 89             | 6.6 %          |

### Centroid-based fits (for comparison)

| subject | HCR c (um) | HCR tilt (°) | HCR rough (um) | HCR frac-above |
|---------|-----------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
| 755252  | 390        | 6.9          | 96             | 9.0 %          |
| 767018  | 315        | 5.0          | 124            | 11.2 %         |
| 767022  | 409        | 3.0          | 84             | 8.4 %          |
| 782149  | 30         | 11.6         | 94             | 15.7 %         |
| 788406  | 248        | 1.2          | 98             | 7.6 %          |
| 790322  | 256        | 4.0          | 85             | 8.5 %          |

Observations:
- CZ pia offset is tight (46–57 um), tilt ≤ 3°, roughness ≤ 12 um — a
  plane fit on the 3D image is essentially perfect.
- HCR pia offset varies widely (18–290 um, the "slanted" remark is
  reflected in tilts up to ~11°). 782149 is the thinnest section — its
  small offset (c=18) means the zarr starts essentially at the pia.
- `fraction-above-pia` is 0–11 % for image-based fits, ~8–16 % for
  centroid-based fits. The remaining positives are mostly out-of-tissue
  segmentation false positives and a few meningeal cells just above the
  cortex proper.
- Depth-from-surface (`depth = z − z_surface(x, y)`) is a robust axial
  coordinate that removes buffer-thickness and pia-tilt confounds.

## Candidate features for automatic coregistration

Measured on all 6 subjects:

- **Cell nearest-neighbor distances** (mean 1-NN, um):
  - CZ: 21.5–24.6; HCR all: 17.1–21.1; HCR GFP+: 18.6–25.0.
  - Characterises packing density; informs k-NN radii for matching.
- **Depth density profiles**: cells/20-um bin vs depth from pia, for CZ, HCR
  total, and HCR GFP+. Cross-correlation (after Z-expansion rescaling) provides
  a 1D axial localization cue. Peaks/troughs in density reflect cortical layer
  structure and can be used as landmarks.
- **Pia surface normal**: an initial-alignment cue independent of cell
  positions; the CZ and HCR surface normals should be aligned after the ~180°
  rotation.
- **GFP+ local density peaks**: candidate constellations for the manual
  workflow's "unique clustered pattern" step.
- **ROI volumes / morphology** (from HCR `cell_body_segmentation/metrics.pickle`
  and CZ segmentation masks): available for per-cell matching confidence.

## Coordinate-system conventions (summary)

| source | column order | units | notes |
|--------|--------------|-------|-------|
| CZ centroid CSV | `[czstack_cell_id, czstack_z, czstack_y, czstack_x]` | pixels (0.78 um XY, 1 um Z) | |
| HCR centroid NPY | `[z, y, x, id]` | pixels (HCR level-2 ~0.988 um XY, 1 um Z) | |
| Landmark CSV | `[ids, active, cz_x, cz_y, cz_z, hcr_x, hcr_y, hcr_z]` | pixels in each modality's native space | no header |
| `spot_488_counts.csv` | `[hcr_id, counts, volume, global_bbox, density]` | 488 GFP feature | only for 782149/788406/790322 |
| `cell_data_mean_*_R1.csv` | channel-indexed intensity means | raw intensity | GFP+ proxy for 755252/767022 |
| Coreg table | `[czstack_id, hcr_id]` | — | final mapping |

## Data-format variations across subjects

- **767018**: uses an older processing pipeline. No `fused_ng.json` (use the
  fallback resolution). HCR cell centroids are in
  `767018_HCR_cell_centroids.csv` in addition to
  `cell_body_segmentation/cell_centroids.npy`. GFP+ is derived by aggregating
  the raw `image_spot_detection/channel_488_spots/spots.csv` on `SEG_ID` at
  load time (no pre-aggregated `spot_488_counts.csv`).
- **755252, 767022**: no `spot_488_counts.csv`. Use
  `/root/capsule/data/cell_data_mean_{subject}_R1.csv` with channel 488 mean
  intensity as GFP+ proxy. Thresholding is required to obtain a true GFP+ set.
- **782149**: thinner HCR section (878 um Z vs 1160–1329 um for others);
  strongest pia tilt (~12°); lowest match rate (~34%).
- **Landmark file naming**: older subjects (755252, 767018, 767022) use a
  `_reordered_qced` suffix; newer ones use `_qced`. Benchmark loaders must
  handle both.

## Testing priorities

- Primary: **788406** and **790322** (highest-quality data, spot-based GFP+).
- Secondary: **767018** (older pipeline) and **782149** (thin section, tilted
  pia).

# Dev protocol
1. Divide complex goals into subgoals.
2. Make a new directory and start a new thread for a each subgoal.
3. Create logs of each subgoals and goals as markdown files to log attempts and results step by step with reasoning.
4. Summarize attempts and results for each subgoal in jupyter notebook format, utilizing plots as much as possible, but only for critical information.
5. Save codes in /root/capsule/code/dev_code, notebooks in /root/capsule/code/notebooks.
6. Number each notebook to show progression.
7. Organize relevant files in /root/capsule/scratch into folders.

## Rule
- Do not use benchmark data information for developing automatic registration.
- Use benchmark dataset for understanding the problem conditions and constraints.
- Use benchmark dataset for validation after the development.

content.text:
<cell id="e49d2b69"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># Automated cell-to-cell matching: what we tried, why it hit a wall

*A plain-English summary of 60+ experimental sessions.*

## The problem in one paragraph

We have two different microscope images of the same piece of mouse brain.
One image (called **CZ**) has about 800 cells, each carefully picked by
hand. The other image (called **HCR**) has 10,000 to 30,000 cells — the
same brain tissue, but chemically expanded to roughly double its size,
rotated 180°, and imaged differently. Our job is to figure out, for
every CZ cell, which HCR cell is the *same physical cell*. Today this
is done by hand; we are trying to automate it.

The two images don't line up cleanly because:

- the expansion isn't uniform (it stretches more in depth than sideways),
- the cells themselves warp as the tissue expands,
- only **a small fraction** of HCR cells have the green marker that
  identifies candidates (roughly 3–10% of them),
- so for each CZ cell, we're looking for one match in a haystack of
  thousands of mostly-wrong neighbours.

We measure success as **"match rate within 20 µm"** — the fraction of
hand-labeled ground-truth cells that the algorithm places within a
cell-diameter of the correct HCR cell.</cell id="e49d2b69">
<cell id="aabbcafa">import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 110
plt.rcParams['axes.grid'] = True
plt.rcParams['grid.alpha'] = 0.3
plt.rcParams['axes.spines.top'] = False
plt.rcParams['axes.spines.right'] = False

# Six real brain samples used for benchmarking.
# Each is a different mouse / section with its own quirks.
SUBJECTS = ['788406', '790322', '755252', '767022', '767018', '782149']
SUBJECT_NOTES = {
    '788406': 'clean, large',
    '790322': 'clean, medium',
    '755252': 'very dense (30k+)',
    '767022': 'dense',
    '767018': 'sparse (9k)',
    '782149': 'thin, tilted',
}</cell id="aabbcafa">
<cell id="e45bbe06"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 1. How well can any single method do it?

We built and tested more than a dozen different matching algorithms.
Some use only the 3D positions of cells (point clouds). Some use the
cell shapes (segmentation masks). Some use the raw image brightness.
Some are learned by neural networks. The plot below shows the
**three strongest single methods**, measured on each of six brain
samples.

- **P1** — a geometric outlier-tolerant matcher (TEASER).
- **P4** — a graph-matching method using cell neighborhoods.
- **P6** — a non-rigid, Bayesian-style warping method (BCPD).

No single method wins across all samples. Different methods are best
for different situations: P1 is strongest on clean, medium-density
brains; P4 is strongest on dense brains; P6 sits in between.</cell id="e45bbe06">
<cell id="98623f60"># From sessions/56_p1_p4_p6_ensemble/three_way_results.csv
p1 = [0.234, 0.251, 0.044, 0.103, 0.143, 0.000]
p4 = [0.173, 0.177, 0.091, 0.048, 0.308, 0.000]
p6 = [0.175, 0.224, 0.052, 0.053, 0.264, 0.000]

x = np.arange(len(SUBJECTS))
w = 0.26
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
ax.bar(x - w, p1, width=w, label='P1 (geometric / TEASER)', color='#1f77b4')
ax.bar(x,     p4, width=w, label='P4 (graph matching)',     color='#ff7f0e')
ax.bar(x + w, p6, width=w, label='P6 (nonrigid / BCPD)',    color='#2ca02c')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f'{s}\n({SUBJECT_NOTES[s]})' for s in SUBJECTS], fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel('match rate within 20 µm\n(fraction of ground-truth cells hit)')
ax.set_title('No single method wins everywhere')
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.45)
ax.legend(loc='upper right', framealpha=0.95)
ax.axhline(0.6, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1)
ax.text(5.3, 0.61, 'target bar (0.6)', color='red', fontsize=8, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()</cell id="98623f60">

Outputs are too large to include. Use Bash with: cat <notebook_path> | jq '.cells[3].outputs'
<cell id="d691c91b"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 2. Combining methods helps — but only up to a point

Since different methods are best on different samples, the natural
next step is to combine them. We built a simple **dispatcher** (call
it **C5**): for each brain, use the method known to work best for
that density of cells. On sparse brains, defer to the method with
highest confidence. On dense brains, prefer the graph matcher.
On medium brains, prefer the geometric matcher.

C5 is our current best. Summing its match rate across all six
brains gives **1.08** (a score between 0 and 6). This is the
"ceiling" for any method that only looks at cell positions.</cell id="d691c91b">
<cell id="cb05bc5f">c5 = [0.263, 0.289, 0.095, 0.117, 0.315, 0.000]
best_single = [max(a, b, c) for a, b, c in zip(p1, p4, p6)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
w = 0.4
ax.bar(x - w/2, best_single, width=w, label='best single method',
       color='#aaaaaa')
ax.bar(x + w/2, c5,          width=w, label='C5 = dispatcher combining P1, P4, P6',
       color='#d62728')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f'{s}\n({SUBJECT_NOTES[s]})' for s in SUBJECTS], fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel('match rate within 20 µm')
ax.set_title(f'C5 (combined) beats best single method on every sample except 782149  —  total = {sum(c5):.2f}')
ax.axhline(0.6, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.45)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()</cell id="cb05bc5f">

Outputs are too large to include. Use Bash with: cat <notebook_path> | jq '.cells[5].outputs'
<cell id="3998f413"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 3. Two brains we still can't match above 0

Look at sample **782149** — every method scores **zero** on it.
Why? Because this brain has three strikes against it:

1. The tissue is **tilted by about 12°** out of plane,
2. The 3D image is **unusually thin** (less than a millimetre deep),
3. Only **34%** of its cells have a matching partner in the other image
   (much lower than the usual 50–85%).

At that combination, there isn't enough geometric signal for *any*
method that only looks at cell positions to find the correct offset.
The search basically has no handles to grip onto. We believe this
sample is **structurally unreachable** without additional information
beyond cell positions (like raw image texture).

The figure below is a schematic of the problem.</cell id="3998f413">
<cell id="d2fb74d0">fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

# Left: normal brain — thick slab, no tilt, good overlap
ax = axes[0]
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.1, 0.3), 0.8, 0.5, fc='#cce5ff', ec='black'))
ax.text(0.5, 0.55, 'normal brain\n(thick, flat,\n~80% overlap)',
        ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title('Typical sample')
ax.axis('off')

# Right: 782149 — thin, tilted, partial overlap
ax = axes[1]
# tilted thin slab
import matplotlib.transforms as mtransforms

            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred'),

<cell id="d5926202"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 4. The consensus-voting idea that *should* have worked but didn't

Our three best methods (P1, P4, P6) agree with each other on some
cells and disagree on others. A reasonable intuition: **when two or
more methods independently pick the same answer, that answer is
probably right.**

We measured this directly. On a single-cell basis:

| sample  | 1 method votes | 2 methods agree | 3 methods agree |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 788406  | 17% correct    | 22% correct     | 28% correct     |
| 790322  | 15% correct    | 34% correct     | 41% correct     |
| 767018  | 17% correct    | 30% correct     | 33% correct     |

So 2-method agreement is genuinely **1.3× to 2.2× more accurate**
than a single vote. The signal is real.</cell id="d5926202">

<cell id="a8d003a6"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>### Why did majority voting lose?

The "2 methods agree → more accurate" statistic is **true on average**,
but it pools together two different situations:

- *All three methods agree* — these picks really are very accurate,
  and C5 already uses them (the priority dispatcher picks the same
  answer).
- *Only two methods agree and the third disagrees* — these are the
  cases where C6 actually **overrides** C5. And here, the disagreeing
  method (P1, the geometric outlier filter) is often **right**, and
  the two agreeing methods share the same **systematic error**.

In other words: when P1 disagrees with P4 and P6, it's usually because
P1's stricter filter has correctly rejected a pair that P4 and P6
agreed on for the wrong reason. Forcing the majority to win throws
away P1's best picks.

**Lesson:** averaging across all 2-vote pairs hides the fact that
the disagreement subset behaves completely differently from the
unanimous subset.</cell id="a8d003a6">
<cell id="118dcce0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 5. The full list of attempts and outcomes

Every bar below is one complete experiment (building, training, benchmarking).
Green = helped, grey = neutral, red = tried and rejected.</cell id="118dcce0">
<cell id="bc311c18">attempts = [
    ('P1 — TEASER geometric',             'green',  'strong on clean brains'),
    ('P4 — spectral graph match',         'green',  'strong on dense brains'),
    ('P6 — BCPD nonrigid',                'green',  'strong on medium brains'),
    ('P3 — RANSAC affine',                'red',    'near-zero recall'),
    ('P5 — Gromov-Wasserstein',           'red',    'collapsed on outliers'),
    ('P14 — Hungarian baseline',          'red',    'no pairwise consistency'),
    ('M1 — mask density NCC',             'red',    'HCR/CZ density mismatch'),
    ('M3 — mask warm-start + centroid',   'red',    'warm-start too weak'),
    ('M4 — per-cell overlap',             'red',    'CZ mask is outline-only'),
    ('I1 — depth intensity profile',      'gray',   'only recovers Z axis'),
    ('I2 — 3D MI affine',                 'gray',   'only a warm-start'),
    ('I3 — MI + B-spline deformable',     'red',    'overshoots, kills matches'),
    ('G1 — learned GNN matcher',          'red',    'synthetic→real transfer gap'),
    ('G2 — contrastive embedding',        'red',    'same transfer gap'),
    ('B1/B2 — seed + TPS expansion',      'gray',   'needs a seed we dont have'),
    ('C1 — image warm-start + P1',        'gray',   'no gain over P1 alone'),
    ('C4 — majority ensemble (old)',      'gray',   'beaten by C5'),
    ('C5 — density-priority dispatcher',  'green',  'CURRENT BEST (1.08)'),

<cell id="61f9a2a3"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 6. Where we are now

Summing across all six benchmark samples:

| what | total match rate (sum over 6 samples) |
|---|---|
| manual (human expert)              | ~6.0 (near-perfect) |
| target automated bar               | 3.6 (0.6 per sample) |
| **C5 — current best automated**    | **1.08**         |
| C6 — consensus-vote attempt        | 1.00 (worse)     |
| best single method (P1)            | 0.74             |

Every lightweight attempt to push past C5 has come back negative:

- confidence calibration (S55): no lift
- B-spline deformable image registration (S57): negative
- learned neural-net matcher (S58, S59): synthetic-to-real gap
- consensus voting (S60, this session): negative

This is a real plateau, not a bug to fix.</cell id="61f9a2a3">
<cell id="84477aa9">fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.2))
bars = ['manual\n(human)', 'target bar', 'C5\n(best automated)', 'C6\nmajority vote', 'best single\nmethod (P1)']
vals = [6.0, 3.6, 1.08, 1.00, 0.74]

for bar, v in zip(b, vals):

            ha='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

Outputs are too large to include. Use Bash with: cat <notebook_path> | jq '.cells[16].outputs'
<cell id="e2645d61"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 7. What's left to try

Three options remain on the table. They split into "probably won't
help" and "expensive but might".

**Probably won't help (low expected value):**

1. **QF1 — learned fallback classifier on top of C5.**
   Would re-rank borderline picks, but doesn't find new ones.
   Calibration experiments (S55) already showed this doesn't lift our
   numbers.

2. **More classical methods** (GeoTransformer, Predator, Lepard, etc.)
   These are learned networks with the same training-data gap that
   killed our G1 attempt: they want real correspondence pairs for
   training, and our only large-scale training data is synthetic HCR
   pairs that don't transfer to CZ↔HCR.

**Expensive but might (medium expected value, high cost):**

3. **C2 — image-based learned matcher.** Instead of just cell positions,
   use the local image texture around each cell as additional signal.
   A 3D convolutional neural network would look at a small image patch
   around every cell and produce a feature that complements the
   geometric features. This is a fundamentally different signal from
   what all our methods so far use, so it could plausibly crack sample
   782149.
   - Needs: GPU, building a full 3D image-patch pipeline, training on
     real CZ↔HCR pairs, validating in multiple sessions.
   - Risk: we don't know whether CZ and HCR images are textually
     similar enough at the cell level for this to transfer.

**Why we stopped here.** The user's original direction was "run
autonomously until you either finish or need more compute
resources." Option 3 crosses that boundary — it needs GPU time and
multi-session investment whose payoff is not certain. Options 1 and
2 are unlikely to move the number. The honest stopping point is now.</cell id="e2645d61">
<cell id="4102ed1a"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## 8. Bottom line

- **C5** (the density-priority dispatcher combining P1, P4, P6) is
  the shippable automated matcher at **1.08** total match rate.
- Sample **782149** appears structurally unreachable with position-only
  signals.
- Every lightweight improvement we could think of has been tried and
  failed.
- The single remaining lever (image-based 3D CNN) is expensive and
  uncertain. Pulling it requires a decision about GPU time and
  multi-session scope.

This is a clean decision point rather than a partial result: either
ship C5 as the automated tier and keep the manual workflow for edge
cases, or authorise the image-based path.</cell id="4102ed1a">

cells.source:
# Automated cell-to-cell matching: what we tried, why it hit a wall

*A plain-English summary of 60+ experimental sessions.*

## The problem in one paragraph

We have two different microscope images of the same piece of mouse brain.
One image (called **CZ**) has about 800 cells, each carefully picked by
hand. The other image (called **HCR**) has 10,000 to 30,000 cells — the
same brain tissue, but chemically expanded to roughly double its size,
rotated 180°, and imaged differently. Our job is to figure out, for
every CZ cell, which HCR cell is the *same physical cell*. Today this
is done by hand; we are trying to automate it.

The two images don't line up cleanly because:

- the expansion isn't uniform (it stretches more in depth than sideways),
- the cells themselves warp as the tissue expands,
- only **a small fraction** of HCR cells have the green marker that
  identifies candidates (roughly 3–10% of them),
- so for each CZ cell, we're looking for one match in a haystack of
  thousands of mostly-wrong neighbours.

We measure success as **"match rate within 20 µm"** — the fraction of
hand-labeled ground-truth cells that the algorithm places within a
cell-diameter of the correct HCR cell.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 110
plt.rcParams['axes.grid'] = True
plt.rcParams['grid.alpha'] = 0.3
plt.rcParams['axes.spines.top'] = False
plt.rcParams['axes.spines.right'] = False

# Six real brain samples used for benchmarking.
# Each is a different mouse / section with its own quirks.
SUBJECTS = ['788406', '790322', '755252', '767022', '767018', '782149']
SUBJECT_NOTES = {
    '788406': 'clean, large',
    '790322': 'clean, medium',
    '755252': 'very dense (30k+)',
    '767022': 'dense',
    '767018': 'sparse (9k)',
    '782149': 'thin, tilted',
}

## 1. How well can any single method do it?

We built and tested more than a dozen different matching algorithms.
Some use only the 3D positions of cells (point clouds). Some use the
cell shapes (segmentation masks). Some use the raw image brightness.
Some are learned by neural networks. The plot below shows the
**three strongest single methods**, measured on each of six brain
samples.

- **P1** — a geometric outlier-tolerant matcher (TEASER).
- **P4** — a graph-matching method using cell neighborhoods.
- **P6** — a non-rigid, Bayesian-style warping method (BCPD).

No single method wins across all samples. Different methods are best
for different situations: P1 is strongest on clean, medium-density
brains; P4 is strongest on dense brains; P6 sits in between.

# From sessions/56_p1_p4_p6_ensemble/three_way_results.csv
p1 = [0.234, 0.251, 0.044, 0.103, 0.143, 0.000]
p4 = [0.173, 0.177, 0.091, 0.048, 0.308, 0.000]
p6 = [0.175, 0.224, 0.052, 0.053, 0.264, 0.000]

x = np.arange(len(SUBJECTS))
w = 0.26
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
ax.bar(x - w, p1, width=w, label='P1 (geometric / TEASER)', color='#1f77b4')
ax.bar(x,     p4, width=w, label='P4 (graph matching)',     color='#ff7f0e')
ax.bar(x + w, p6, width=w, label='P6 (nonrigid / BCPD)',    color='#2ca02c')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f'{s}\n({SUBJECT_NOTES[s]})' for s in SUBJECTS], fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel('match rate within 20 µm\n(fraction of ground-truth cells hit)')
ax.set_title('No single method wins everywhere')
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.45)
ax.legend(loc='upper right', framealpha=0.95)
ax.axhline(0.6, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1)
ax.text(5.3, 0.61, 'target bar (0.6)', color='red', fontsize=8, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

cells.source:
## 2. Combining methods helps — but only up to a point

Since different methods are best on different samples, the natural
next step is to combine them. We built a simple **dispatcher** (call
it **C5**): for each brain, use the method known to work best for
that density of cells. On sparse brains, defer to the method with
highest confidence. On dense brains, prefer the graph matcher.
On medium brains, prefer the geometric matcher.

C5 is our current best. Summing its match rate across all six
brains gives **1.08** (a score between 0 and 6). This is the
"ceiling" for any method that only looks at cell positions.

c5 = [0.263, 0.289, 0.095, 0.117, 0.315, 0.000]
best_single = [max(a, b, c) for a, b, c in zip(p1, p4, p6)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
w = 0.4
ax.bar(x - w/2, best_single, width=w, label='best single method',
       color='#aaaaaa')
ax.bar(x + w/2, c5,          width=w, label='C5 = dispatcher combining P1, P4, P6',
       color='#d62728')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f'{s}\n({SUBJECT_NOTES[s]})' for s in SUBJECTS], fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel('match rate within 20 µm')
ax.set_title(f'C5 (combined) beats best single method on every sample except 782149  —  total = {sum(c5):.2f}')
ax.axhline(0.6, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.45)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

cells.source:
## 3. Two brains we still can't match above 0

Look at sample **782149** — every method scores **zero** on it.
Why? Because this brain has three strikes against it:

1. The tissue is **tilted by about 12°** out of plane,
2. The 3D image is **unusually thin** (less than a millimetre deep),
3. Only **34%** of its cells have a matching partner in the other image
   (much lower than the usual 50–85%).

At that combination, there isn't enough geometric signal for *any*
method that only looks at cell positions to find the correct offset.
The search basically has no handles to grip onto. We believe this
sample is **structurally unreachable** without additional information
beyond cell positions (like raw image texture).

The figure below is a schematic of the problem.

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

# Left: normal brain — thick slab, no tilt, good overlap
ax = axes[0]
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.1, 0.3), 0.8, 0.5, fc='#cce5ff', ec='black'))
ax.text(0.5, 0.55, 'normal brain\n(thick, flat,\n~80% overlap)',
        ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title('Typical sample')
ax.axis('off')

# Right: 782149 — thin, tilted, partial overlap
ax = axes[1]
# tilted thin slab
import matplotlib.transforms as mtransforms
rect = plt.Rectangle((0.15, 0.45), 0.7, 0.15, fc='#ffcccc', ec='black')
tr = mtransforms.Affine2D().rotate_deg_around(0.5, 0.525, 12) + ax.transData
rect.set_transform(tr)
ax.add_patch(rect)
ax.annotate('12° tilt', xy=(0.86, 0.55), xytext=(0.95, 0.7),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred'),
            fontsize=9, color='darkred', ha='right')
ax.annotate('thin (<1 mm)', xy=(0.5, 0.47), xytext=(0.5, 0.15),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred'),
            fontsize=9, color='darkred', ha='center')
ax.text(0.5, 0.87, '782149: only 34% of cells\nhave a partner',
        ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10, color='darkred')
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title('The hard sample')
ax.axis('off')

plt.suptitle('Why sample 782149 breaks every position-only method', fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

  cases where C6 actually **overrides** C5. And here, the disagreeing
  method (P1, the geometric outlier filter) is often **right**, and
  the two agreeing methods share the same **systematic error**.

In other words: when P1 disagrees with P4 and P6, it's usually because
P1's stricter filter has correctly rejected a pair that P4 and P6
agreed on for the wrong reason. Forcing the majority to win throws
away P1's best picks.

**Lesson:** averaging across all 2-vote pairs hides the fact that
the disagreement subset behaves completely differently from the
unanimous subset.

## 5. The full list of attempts and outcomes

Every bar below is one complete experiment (building, training, benchmarking).
Green = helped, grey = neutral, red = tried and rejected.

attempts = [
    ('P1 — TEASER geometric',             'green',  'strong on clean brains'),
    ('P4 — spectral graph match',         'green',  'strong on dense brains'),
    ('P6 — BCPD nonrigid',                'green',  'strong on medium brains'),
    ('P3 — RANSAC affine',                'red',    'near-zero recall'),
    ('P5 — Gromov-Wasserstein',           'red',    'collapsed on outliers'),
    ('P14 — Hungarian baseline',          'red',    'no pairwise consistency'),
    ('M1 — mask density NCC',             'red',    'HCR/CZ density mismatch'),
    ('M3 — mask warm-start + centroid',   'red',    'warm-start too weak'),
    ('M4 — per-cell overlap',             'red',    'CZ mask is outline-only'),
    ('I1 — depth intensity profile',      'gray',   'only recovers Z axis'),
    ('I2 — 3D MI affine',                 'gray',   'only a warm-start'),
    ('I3 — MI + B-spline deformable',     'red',    'overshoots, kills matches'),
    ('G1 — learned GNN matcher',          'red',    'synthetic→real transfer gap'),
    ('G2 — contrastive embedding',        'red',    'same transfer gap'),
    ('B1/B2 — seed + TPS expansion',      'gray',   'needs a seed we dont have'),
    ('C1 — image warm-start + P1',        'gray',   'no gain over P1 alone'),
    ('C4 — majority ensemble (old)',      'gray',   'beaten by C5'),
    ('C5 — density-priority dispatcher',  'green',  'CURRENT BEST (1.08)'),
    ('C6 — consensus-vote override',      'red',    'tested this session — loses'),
    ('C6b — consensus + fair fallback',   'red',    'tested this session — loses'),
    ('F7 — confidence calibration',       'gray',   'calibrates but no lift'),
    ('F8 — synthetic-warp training data', 'gray',   'trains, doesnt transfer'),
]

colors = {'green': '#2ca02c', 'red': '#d62728', 'gray': '#b0b0b0'}
labels = [a[0] for a in attempts]
outcomes = [a[2] for a in attempts]
cs = [colors[a[1]] for a in attempts]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 8))
y = np.arange(len(labels))[::-1]
ax.barh(y, [1]*len(labels), color=cs, alpha=0.8, edgecolor='white')
for yi, lab, outc in zip(y, labels, outcomes):
    ax.text(0.02, yi, lab, va='center', fontsize=9, color='white', fontweight='bold')
    ax.text(0.55, yi, outc, va='center', fontsize=9, color='black')
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_visible(False)

legend = [mpatches.Patch(color=colors[c], label=l) for c, l in
          [('green', 'contributes to the current best'),
           ('gray',  'neutral / infrastructure'),
           ('red',   'tried and rejected')]]
ax.legend(handles=legend, loc='lower right', framealpha=1)
ax.set_title('22 method attempts across point-cloud, mask, image, hybrid, and learned families')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

## 6. Where we are now

Summing across all six benchmark samples:

| what | total match rate (sum over 6 samples) |
|---|---|
| manual (human expert)              | ~6.0 (near-perfect) |
| target automated bar               | 3.6 (0.6 per sample) |
| **C5 — current best automated**    | **1.08**         |
| C6 — consensus-vote attempt        | 1.00 (worse)     |
| best single method (P1)            | 0.74             |

Every lightweight attempt to push past C5 has come back negative:

- confidence calibration (S55): no lift
- B-spline deformable image registration (S57): negative
- learned neural-net matcher (S58, S59): synthetic-to-real gap
- consensus voting (S60, this session): negative

This is a real plateau, not a bug to fix.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.2))
bars = ['manual\n(human)', 'target bar', 'C5\n(best automated)', 'C6\nmajority vote', 'best single\nmethod (P1)']
vals = [6.0, 3.6, 1.08, 1.00, 0.74]
cs2 = ['#2ca02c', '#bdbdbd', '#d62728', '#9467bd', '#1f77b4']
b = ax.bar(bars, vals, color=cs2)
for bar, v in zip(b, vals):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, v + 0.08, f'{v:.2f}',
            ha='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('sum of match rates across 6 samples\n(max possible = 6)')
ax.set_ylim(0, 6.5)
ax.set_title('We are at 1.08; target is 3.6. The gap is real.')
ax.axhline(3.6, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4, linewidth=1)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

## 7. What's left to try

Three options remain on the table. They split into "probably won't
help" and "expensive but might".

**Probably won't help (low expected value):**

1. **QF1 — learned fallback classifier on top of C5.**
   Would re-rank borderline picks, but doesn't find new ones.
   Calibration experiments (S55) already showed this doesn't lift our
   numbers.

2. **More classical methods** (GeoTransformer, Predator, Lepard, etc.)
   These are learned networks with the same training-data gap that
   killed our G1 attempt: they want real correspondence pairs for
   training, and our only large-scale training data is synthetic HCR
   pairs that don't transfer to CZ↔HCR.

**Expensive but might (medium expected value, high cost):**

3. **C2 — image-based learned matcher.** Instead of just cell positions,
   use the local image texture around each cell as additional signal.
   A 3D convolutional neural network would look at a small image patch
   around every cell and produce a feature that complements the
   geometric features. This is a fundamentally different signal from
   what all our methods so far use, so it could plausibly crack sample
   782149.
   - Needs: GPU, building a full 3D image-patch pipeline, training on
     real CZ↔HCR pairs, validating in multiple sessions.
   - Risk: we don't know whether CZ and HCR images are textually
     similar enough at the cell level for this to transfer.

**Why we stopped here.** The user's original direction was "run
autonomously until you either finish or need more compute
resources." Option 3 crosses that boundary — it needs GPU time and
multi-session investment whose payoff is not certain. Options 1 and
2 are unlikely to move the number. The honest stopping point is now.

## 8. Bottom line

- **C5** (the density-priority dispatcher combining P1, P4, P6) is
  the shippable automated matcher at **1.08** total match rate.
- Sample **782149** appears structurally unreachable with position-only
  signals.
- Every lightweight improvement we could think of has been tried and
  failed.
- The single remaining lever (image-based 3D CNN) is expensive and
  uncertain. Pulling it requires a decision about GPU time and
  multi-session scope.

This is a clean decision point rather than a partial result: either
ship C5 as the automated tier and keep the manual workflow for edge
cases, or authorise the image-based path.

1	# Session 03c — Intensity-feature extraction at N22 surface onset
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-19
4	Author: Claude (autonomous research loop)
5	
6	## Goal
7	
8	The image-only v2.1 pia surface estimator
9	(`03_image_based_surface.py`) matches N22 (centroid-based) on 5/6 HCR
10	subjects but catastrophically fails on **755252** (onset_depth
11	62.5 µm vs N22's 2.5 µm).  The failure mode is a thick
12	autofluorescence sheet ~200 µm above the real pia that is
13	**indistinguishable from a thin pia edge using column-profile
14	statistics alone** (see `../03_image_based_surface_estimation/log.md`
15	for the v2.1 negative-results audit).
16	
17	**Hypothesis:** N22's surface, derived from centroid positions, gives
18	us a reliable per-subject "ground truth" pia z for every (x, y).
19	Intensity features extracted in a *z-window* around the N22 surface
20	should differ measurably from intensity features at the v2.1
21	image-based surface, *only on 755252* (where v2.1 sits on AF, not
22	pia).  That difference is the signal we need.
23	
24	**Success criterion:** Discover at least one feature whose
25	distribution at the N22 surface is consistent across subjects AND
26	clearly separates from the distribution at the AF-contaminated
27	v2.1 surface on 755252.
28	
29	## Iteration structure
30	
31	Each iteration produces:
32	- a **notebook** in `iterations/iterNN_*.ipynb` with plots and tables
33	- a brief **iteration log** appended below
34	- **figures** in `figures/iterNN_*.png`
35	- **data** in `data/iterNN_*.parquet` or `.npz` when caching helps
36	
37	## Iterations
38	
39	### Iteration 1 — Intensity features at N22 vs v2.1 surface
40	
41	**Setup.**  For each of the 6 HCR subjects load the combined
42	(`load_hcr_combined`) volume, run `estimate_pia_surface_quantile_ceiling`
43	to get the N22 surface, and run `estimate_surface_and_l2_image_based`
44	(v2.1, `target_quantile = 0.85`) to get the image-only surface.
45	Sample a 20 × 20 grid of interior columns (edge pad 15 %), and for
46	each column compute features anchored at both `z_N22` and `z_v21`.
47	
48	**Features.**  Means in windows relative to the surface z:
49	`above_near = [z-50,z-10]`, `below_near = [z+10,z+50]`,
50	`below_mid = [z+50,z+150]`, `below_deep = [z+150,z+250]`; central
51	gradient at z (±10 µm); a "dip" trio (peak in [z,z+30], trough in
52	[z+30,z+150], peak-minus-trough).
53	
54	**Artefacts.**
55	- `iterations/iter01_compute.py` — computation driver
56	- `iterations/iter01_analysis.ipynb` — rendered figures + tables
57	- `data/iter01_features.csv` — 2400 rows (6 × 400 columns)
58	- `data/iter01_profiles.npz` — sample column profiles for plots
59	- `figures/iter01_sample_profiles.png` — center-column profiles with
60	  both surfaces overlaid per subject
61	- `figures/iter01_below_near_hist.png` — per-subject histograms
62	- `figures/iter01_ratio_near_deep_hist.png` — scale-free variant
63	
64	**Key finding.**  `below_near` (mean intensity in a 40 µm slab
65	immediately below the candidate surface) cleanly discriminates
66	755252's AF-top v21 position from its real-pia N22 position:
67	
68	| subject | median at N22 | median at v21 |
69	|---|---:|---:|
70	| **755252** | **0.052** | **0.000**  ← AF |
71	| 767018    | 0.015 | 0.008 |
72	| 767022    | 0.015 | 0.007 |
73	| 782149    | 0.030 | 0.008 |
74	| 788406    | 0.002 | 0.001 |
75	| 790322    | 0.001 | 0.001 |
76	
77	Physical reading: on 755252 the v21 surface sits on top of a ~30–50 µm
78	thick AF sheet with a **dark gap** between the AF and the real pia.
79	The 40 µm slab below v21 falls in that gap; its median intensity is
80	floored at 0 by the `load_hcr_combined` normalisation (values below
81	the per-channel median are clipped to 0).  At the real pia (N22), the
82	same slab contains bulk tissue and is clearly non-zero.
83	
84	**Caveats.**
85	- Median-based summary smears per-column variance; per-column
86	  discrimination is what the estimator will actually consume.
87	  Next iteration needs to compute the feature per column top (Stage
88	  3 of the v2.1 pipeline) and show that AF tops and pia tops are
89	  separable at the individual-column level.
90	- Several features (`above_near`, `dip_trough`, etc.) came back as
91	  exact zeros, because `load_hcr_combined` clips sub-median voxels
92	  to 0. For iteration 2 we should switch to raw per-channel
93	  intensities (or at least a non-clipping normalisation) so small
94	  differences in "dim" regions are preserved.
95	
96	**Decision.**  Iteration 2 will (i) compute `below_near` per column
97	top in Stage 3 of the v2.1 pipeline, (ii) check that AF tops and
98	pia tops form separable distributions, and (iii) design a per-top
99	acceptance rule (soft weighting or hard rejection) before the IRLS
100	quantile fit.
101	
102	### Iteration 2 — Per-column-top `below_near` is *not* discriminative
103	
104	**Setup.**  Replicated v2.1 Stage 2 + 3 for each subject with the
105	`03_image_based_surface.py` defaults; labelled each column top as
106	`pia` (|z_top − z_N22(x,y)| ≤ 25 µm) or `AF/other` (else); measured
107	`below_near` at every top.  See `iterations/iter02_compute.py` +
108	`iterations/iter02_analysis.ipynb`.
109	
110	**Finding.**  Per-top `below_near` is **not separable** between pia
111	and AF tops.  On 755252 the very few tops that fell in the AF zone
112	(dist_to_n22 ∈ [−50, −10], n=89) had `below_near` ≈ 0.30, while
113	pia-labelled tops (n=865) had `below_near` ≈ 0.25 and deeper
114	tissue-internal tops (dist ∈ [+25, +75]) had 0.18.  All are
115	"bright-just-below" because the probed slab sits inside either
116	AF body (AF top) or tissue (pia / deep top); the feature cannot tell
117	them apart at the column level.
118	
119	**Why iter-1 still worked.**  The iter-1 signal was specific to
120	**the fitted surface z**, not to individual column tops.  v2.1's
121	fitted surface on 755252 ends up ~60–90 µm above N22, landing in
122	the *gap* between the AF bottom and the real pia onset.  That gap
123	is narrow (≈ 40 µm on 755252) and individual column tops rarely sit
124	inside it — but the fitted surface does, and in that gap `below_near`
125	collapses to 0.
126	
127	**Artefacts.**
128	- `iterations/iter02_compute.py` — extractor
129	- `iterations/iter02_analysis.ipynb` — distributions + tables
130	- `data/iter02_tops.csv` (140 712 rows)
131	- `figures/iter02_top_below_near_hist.png` — overlapping pia/AF histograms
132	- `figures/iter02_dist_to_n22_hist.png` — distribution of z_top−z_N22 per subject
133	
134	**Decision.**  Don't filter individual tops.  Iteration 3 will score
135	**entire candidate surfaces** by their aggregate `below_near` (or a
136	scale-free variant) and use that score to choose among candidates
137	(different channel subsets, different `target_quantile`).
138	
139	
140	### Iteration 3 — Surface quality score + candidate-bank auto-selection
141	
142	**Setup.**  For each subject, fit v2.1 on 7 candidate (channel-subset,
143	target_quantile) combinations.  Also compute N22 and v2.1-default for
144	reference.  Score every candidate in the reference combined volume on
145	a 20 × 20 interior grid (400 columns) using three features:
146	
147	- `Q      = median below_near`              (mean intensity 10–50 µm below surface)
148	- `Qabove = median above_near`              (mean intensity 10–50 µm above surface)
149	- `Qcon_n = median ((b - a) / (b + a + ε))` (normalised contrast in [-1, 1])
150

# Session 03c — Intensity-feature extraction at N22 surface onset

Date: 2026-04-19
Author: Claude (autonomous research loop)

## Goal

The image-only v2.1 pia surface estimator
(`03_image_based_surface.py`) matches N22 (centroid-based) on 5/6 HCR
subjects but catastrophically fails on **755252** (onset_depth
62.5 µm vs N22's 2.5 µm).  The failure mode is a thick
autofluorescence sheet ~200 µm above the real pia that is
**indistinguishable from a thin pia edge using column-profile
statistics alone** (see `../03_image_based_surface_estimation/log.md`
for the v2.1 negative-results audit).

**Hypothesis:** N22's surface, derived from centroid positions, gives
us a reliable per-subject "ground truth" pia z for every (x, y).
Intensity features extracted in a *z-window* around the N22 surface
should differ measurably from intensity features at the v2.1
image-based surface, *only on 755252* (where v2.1 sits on AF, not
pia).  That difference is the signal we need.

**Success criterion:** Discover at least one feature whose
distribution at the N22 surface is consistent across subjects AND
clearly separates from the distribution at the AF-contaminated
v2.1 surface on 755252.

## Iteration structure

Each iteration produces:
- a **notebook** in `iterations/iterNN_*.ipynb` with plots and tables
- a brief **iteration log** appended below
- **figures** in `figures/iterNN_*.png`
- **data** in `data/iterNN_*.parquet` or `.npz` when caching helps

## Iterations

### Iteration 1 — Intensity features at N22 vs v2.1 surface

**Setup.**  For each of the 6 HCR subjects load the combined
(`load_hcr_combined`) volume, run `estimate_pia_surface_quantile_ceiling`
to get the N22 surface, and run `estimate_surface_and_l2_image_based`
(v2.1, `target_quantile = 0.85`) to get the image-only surface.
Sample a 20 × 20 grid of interior columns (edge pad 15 %), and for
each column compute features anchored at both `z_N22` and `z_v21`.

**Features.**  Means in windows relative to the surface z:
`above_near = [z-50,z-10]`, `below_near = [z+10,z+50]`,
`below_mid = [z+50,z+150]`, `below_deep = [z+150,z+250]`; central
gradient at z (±10 µm); a "dip" trio (peak in [z,z+30], trough in
[z+30,z+150], peak-minus-trough).

**Artefacts.**
- `iterations/iter01_compute.py` — computation driver
- `iterations/iter01_analysis.ipynb` — rendered figures + tables
- `data/iter01_features.csv` — 2400 rows (6 × 400 columns)
- `data/iter01_profiles.npz` — sample column profiles for plots
- `figures/iter01_sample_profiles.png` — center-column profiles with
  both surfaces overlaid per subject
- `figures/iter01_below_near_hist.png` — per-subject histograms
- `figures/iter01_ratio_near_deep_hist.png` — scale-free variant

**Key finding.**  `below_near` (mean intensity in a 40 µm slab
immediately below the candidate surface) cleanly discriminates
755252's AF-top v21 position from its real-pia N22 position:

| subject | median at N22 | median at v21 |
|---|---:|---:|
| **755252** | **0.052** | **0.000**  ← AF |
| 767018    | 0.015 | 0.008 |
| 767022    | 0.015 | 0.007 |
| 782149    | 0.030 | 0.008 |
| 788406    | 0.002 | 0.001 |
| 790322    | 0.001 | 0.001 |

Physical reading: on 755252 the v21 surface sits on top of a ~30–50 µm
thick AF sheet with a **dark gap** between the AF and the real pia.
The 40 µm slab below v21 falls in that gap; its median intensity is
floored at 0 by the `load_hcr_combined` normalisation (values below
the per-channel median are clipped to 0).  At the real pia (N22), the
same slab contains bulk tissue and is clearly non-zero.

**Caveats.**
- Median-based summary smears per-column variance; per-column
  discrimination is what the estimator will actually consume.
  Next iteration needs to compute the feature per column top (Stage
  3 of the v2.1 pipeline) and show that AF tops and pia tops are
  separable at the individual-column level.
- Several features (`above_near`, `dip_trough`, etc.) came back as
  exact zeros, because `load_hcr_combined` clips sub-median voxels
  to 0. For iteration 2 we should switch to raw per-channel
  intensities (or at least a non-clipping normalisation) so small
  differences in "dim" regions are preserved.

**Decision.**  Iteration 2 will (i) compute `below_near` per column
top in Stage 3 of the v2.1 pipeline, (ii) check that AF tops and
pia tops form separable distributions, and (iii) design a per-top
acceptance rule (soft weighting or hard rejection) before the IRLS
quantile fit.

### Iteration 2 — Per-column-top `below_near` is *not* discriminative

**Setup.**  Replicated v2.1 Stage 2 + 3 for each subject with the
`03_image_based_surface.py` defaults; labelled each column top as
`pia` (|z_top − z_N22(x,y)| ≤ 25 µm) or `AF/other` (else); measured
`below_near` at every top.  See `iterations/iter02_compute.py` +
`iterations/iter02_analysis.ipynb`.

**Finding.**  Per-top `below_near` is **not separable** between pia
and AF tops.  On 755252 the very few tops that fell in the AF zone
(dist_to_n22 ∈ [−50, −10], n=89) had `below_near` ≈ 0.30, while
pia-labelled tops (n=865) had `below_near` ≈ 0.25 and deeper
tissue-internal tops (dist ∈ [+25, +75]) had 0.18.  All are
"bright-just-below" because the probed slab sits inside either
AF body (AF top) or tissue (pia / deep top); the feature cannot tell
them apart at the column level.

**Why iter-1 still worked.**  The iter-1 signal was specific to
**the fitted surface z**, not to individual column tops.  v2.1's
fitted surface on 755252 ends up ~60–90 µm above N22, landing in
the *gap* between the AF bottom and the real pia onset.  That gap
is narrow (≈ 40 µm on 755252) and individual column tops rarely sit
inside it — but the fitted surface does, and in that gap `below_near`
collapses to 0.

**Artefacts.**
- `iterations/iter02_compute.py` — extractor
- `iterations/iter02_analysis.ipynb` — distributions + tables
- `data/iter02_tops.csv` (140 712 rows)
- `figures/iter02_top_below_near_hist.png` — overlapping pia/AF histograms
- `figures/iter02_dist_to_n22_hist.png` — distribution of z_top−z_N22 per subject

**Decision.**  Don't filter individual tops.  Iteration 3 will score
**entire candidate surfaces** by their aggregate `below_near` (or a
scale-free variant) and use that score to choose among candidates
(different channel subsets, different `target_quantile`).


### Iteration 3 — Surface quality score + candidate-bank auto-selection

**Setup.**  For each subject, fit v2.1 on 7 candidate (channel-subset,
target_quantile) combinations.  Also compute N22 and v2.1-default for
reference.  Score every candidate in the reference combined volume on
a 20 × 20 interior grid (400 columns) using three features:

- `Q      = median below_near`              (mean intensity 10–50 µm below surface)
- `Qabove = median above_near`              (mean intensity 10–50 µm above surface)
- `Qcon_n = median ((b - a) / (b + a + ε))` (normalised contrast in [-1, 1])
